coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots optimize the luminescence and morphological of Tm3+ doped zinc borate composites for NUV-WLEDs
coredata.dc:creator: He, Lili
Liu, Rui
Ge, Chunhua
Yang, Li
Jia, Kai
Zhang, Xiangdong
coredata.dc:description: Rare earth (RE)-doped luminescent materials have shown tremendous potential applications in the optical field. However, it is still a challenge to improve its luminescence properties further. In this study, the Tm3+-doped zinc borate (4ZnO·B2O3·H2O, ZBH) with a flaky-like morphology was successfully prepared by a facile hydrothermal reaction. The photoluminescence (PL) performance of ZBH:Tm3+ is improved through incorporating yellow N-doped carbon dots (yNCDs). Simultaneously, by changing the content of yNCDs in ZBH:Tm3+ host, the morphological evolution from flaky-like to flower-like of the ZBH:Tm3+/yNCDs composite are realized. The ZBH:Tm3+/yNCDs composite with flower-like exhibits the strongest white light emission and splendid thermal stability under 360 nm excitation wavelength, matching the current mainstream near-ultraviolet (NUV) LED chip. Thus, ZBH:Tm3+/yNCDs composite might be a promising luminescent material in the NUV white lighting and display fields. The findings of this study developed a novel idea for improving the luminescent properties of RE-doped materials. Besides, it also solves fluorescence quenching problems of carbon dots in the solid-state illumination field.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Rare earth ions
Zinc borate
Morphology
White light-emitting diodes

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Correlation of dopant content, composites morphology and luminescence intensity

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of the Education Department of Liaoning Provincial

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The present project was supported by Natural Science Foundation of the Education Department of Liaoning Provincial ( LJC201916 and LFW201901 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FT-IR spectra of ZBH, ZBH:9%Tm3+, yNCDs and ZBH:9%Tm3+/yNCDs (1–7) samples.
XRD patterns of ZBH, ZBH:9%Tm3+, yNCDs and ZBH:9%Tm3+/yNCDs (1–7) samples.
SEM images of ZBH, ZBH:9%Tm3+ and ZBH:9%Tm3+/yNCDs (1–7) samples.
(a) SEM images, (b) selected EDS spectra of a typical flower-like ZBH:9%Tm3+/yNCDs (5), and EDS mapping of B, C, N, O, Zn, and Tm.
(a) PLE spectra of ZBH:9%Tm3+ sample under 454 nm emission wavelength. (b) PL spectra of ZBH:xTm3+ (x = 0–15 mol%) samples, the inset is the relevance between PL intensity and Tm3+ doping amount.
(a) PL spectra of ZBH:9%Tm3+/yNCDs (1–7) samples. (b) The relevance between PL intensity and yNCDs doping amount.
(a) PL spectra of ZBH:9%Tm3+/yNCDs (5) samples under diverse temperature surroundings, and (b) graph of PL intensity under diverse temperature surroundings (λex = 360 nm).
CIE chromaticity diagram of ZBH:9%Tm3+, yNCDs, and ZBH:9%Tm3+/yNCDs (1–7) samples. The illustration is an optical image of the WLEDs device.
Schematic illustration for growth process of flower-like ZBH:9%Tm3+/yNCDs.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots optimize the luminescence and morphological of Tm3+ doped zinc borate composites for NUV-WLEDs

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, Chongshan Road No. 66, Shenyang 110036, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, Chongshan Road No. 66, Shenyang 110036, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Academy of Forensic Science, Liaoning University, Chongshan Road No. 66, Shenyang 110036, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Academy of Forensic Science, Liaoning University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Academy of forensic science, Liaoning University, Chongshan Road No. 66, Shenyang 110036, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rare earth (RE)-doped luminescent materials have shown tremendous potential applications in the optical field. However, it is still a challenge to improve its luminescence properties further. In this study, the Tm3+-doped zinc borate (4ZnO·B2O3·H2O, ZBH) with a flaky-like morphology was successfully prepared by a facile hydrothermal reaction. The photoluminescence (PL) performance of ZBH:Tm3+ is improved through incorporating yellow N-doped carbon dots (yNCDs). Simultaneously, by changing the content of yNCDs in ZBH:Tm3+ host, the morphological evolution from flaky-like to flower-like of the ZBH:Tm3+/yNCDs composite are realized. The ZBH:Tm3+/yNCDs composite with flower-like exhibits the strongest white light emission and splendid thermal stability under 360 nm excitation wavelength, matching the current mainstream near-ultraviolet (NUV) LED chip. Thus, ZBH:Tm3+/yNCDs composite might be a promising luminescent material in the NUV white lighting and display fields. The findings of this study developed a novel idea for improving the luminescent properties of RE-doped materials. Besides, it also solves fluorescence quenching problems of carbon dots in the solid-state illumination field.

ce:section.ce:para: White light-emitting diodes (WLEDs) will become the dominant solid-state lighting in the future on account of its excellent performance, such as high brightness, low power consumption, environmental protection, and long life [1–3]. Contemporarily, most commercial WLEDs are constituted by blue LED chips and one or more phosphors that emit complementary colors. However, the main disadvantage of this type of WLEDs is the low color rendering index (CRI) and poor color stability by virtue of the different degradation rates of individual phosphors. Simultaneously, the intense blue emission peaks appear in the white light spectrum, causing retinal damage and adversely influence health status [4,5]. Recently, the direct excitation of white light-emitting phosphors by near-ultraviolet (NUV) LED chips are considered to be the most promising combination of WLEDs, which could expect to overcome the drawbacks of multicolor phosphors, alleviate the hazard of blue light to eyes, and effectively improve CRI [6–8]. Accordingly, the design of novel high-quality white light emission phosphors will promote the development and performance optimization of WLEDs.

Carbon dots (CDs) is an excellent fluorescent material that has gained heightened attention owing to its low cost, low toxicity, good biocompatibility, and unique photoluminescence (PL) [9–12]. Doping and surface functionalization are the main methods to improve CDs' PL properties [13,14]. Nitrogen (N) is a typical dopant. In N-doped CDs (NCDs), N atoms provide excess electrons as n-type impurities, causing the Fermi level to move upward and changing PL performances, for instance, increasing quantum yield and regulating emission wavelength [15–17]. For the past few years, CDs have become a potential phosphor candidate as important as rare earth (RE)-doped compounds, perovskites, and metal-organic frameworks [18–20]. Nevertheless, solid-state CDs are prone to luminescence quenching due to the aggregation-induced quenching (AIQ) effects, which seriously hindered the applications in solid-state lighting [21–23]. Matrix encapsulation is a simple and high-efficiency strategy to prevent the adverse AIQ effect of CDs. The investigations have confirmed that solid-state fluorescence based on CDs composites could be achieved by embedding CDs in a solid matrix, which includes xerogel [24], starch [25], the polymer [26,27], borax [28], and so on. In these circumstances, the CDs are uniformly dispersed and protected in the solid matrix. Interestingly, embedding CDs in the matrix not only successfully overcomes its fluorescence quenching but maintains the primary performance of CDs. Simultaneously, CDs/matrix composites' photoluminescence properties can be further adjusted to endow the original material with new properties, thereby extending the application field [29]. Whereas, the current research on CDs-based solid materials is still in its infancy and requires further exploration. Hence, it is crucial to discover suitable matrix materials to develop more CDs-based composites in practical applications.

In recent years, zinc borate as a matrix of photoluminescence materials with the advantages of low synthesis temperature, excellent chemical stability, controllable functionalization, and low cost are highly desired [30,31]. Generally, zinc borate possesses only weak blue emission. In view of practicality, it is hoped that the prepared material has higher luminescence performance. At present, RE-doped zinc borate is an essential method to obtain ideal phosphors due to the controllable morphology and tunable luminescence [32,33]. But the principal shortcoming of RE ions as phosphors is the low molar absorptivity related to the parity forbidden 4 f→4 f transitions and the narrow absorption band, which results in inefficient fluorescence excitation and greatly limits the emission intensity [34,35]. Previous literature reports have proposed several feasible strategies to overcome these disadvantages for RE-doped materials, including co-doping with other RE ions, combining with semiconductor quantum dots, and utilizing the “antenna effect” of organic ligands, etc [36]. To date, it has been desired to develop a novel method to improve RE ions' optical performance. Based on existing technology, the composite materials formation could effectively enhance RE ions’ luminescent properties in phosphors [37]. However, there are reports scarcely on using fluorescent CDs to improve RE-doped nano/micro-materials’ luminescence performance.

In the previous work, we have successfully introduced yellow N-doped carbon dots (yNCDs) into zinc borate (4ZnO·B2O3·H2O, ZBH) matrix and obtained ZBH/yNCDs composite material [38]. In this work, Tm3+ and yNCDs (Tm3+/yNCDs) were simultaneously introduced into the ZBH matrix by hydrothermal method, and white light-emitting composites (ZBH:Tm3+/yNCDs) with different morphologies were fabricated. Compared with ZBH/yNCDs and ZBH:Tm3+, the morphology and PL properties of ZBH:Tm3+/yNCDs composites changed significantly. The effects of Tm3+/yNCDs contents on the structure, morphology, and PL properties of the ZBH:Tm3+/yNCDs composites were discussed. The relationship between luminescent intensity and different phosphors morphology was studied, and the growth mechanism of flower-like morphology was proposed. This report develops a new synthesis technique for NUV-WLEDs phosphors, which proposes a feasible route to enhance the RE-doped materials’ luminescence performance and successfully overcomes the CDs’ AIQ effect in the solid-state as well.

Boric acid (H3BO3, 99.9%), zinc vitriol (ZnSO4·7H2O, 99.9%), ammonia water (NH3·H2O, 25 wt%), and anhydrous ethanol (C2H5OH, 75.0%) were purchased from Beijing Chemicals Co. Thulium oxide (Tm2O3, 99.9%) and o-phenylenediamine (o-PD, 99.9%) were received from Aladdin. All chemicals were used without any treatment.

4ZnO·B2O3·H2O:Tm3+ composites (ZBH:Tm3+) were prepared via a simple hydrothermal reaction. 0.4084 g of H3BO3 and 0.5756 g of ZnSO4·7H2O were dissolved in distilled water. Add a certain amount of Tm2O3 to the above solution, and continuously stirred for 40 min. Subsequently, adjust the mixed solution's pH to about 7 with NH3·H2O to obtain the sol-gel solution. Continue stirring for 60 min, and then transfer the sol-gel solution to a Teflon-lined autoclave. After reacting under 180 °C for 72 h, filter and alternately wash several times at normal pressure. Finally, ZBH:Tm3+ solid powders were collected through oven-dry processing. By controlling the amount of Tm2O3, a series of ZBH:xTm3+ (x = 0, 3, 6, 9, 12, and 15 mol%) samples were fabricated. Among them, ZBH:xTm3+ (x = 0 mol%) sample is a pure ZBH.

The yNCDs solid powders were prepared using o-PD as a carbon source, according to the previously reported hydrothermal synthesis method [39]. The synthesis procedure of ZBH:9%Tm3+/yNCDs samples was similar to ZBH:Tm3+. Before transferring the sol-gel solution to the Teflon-lined autoclave, a series of yNCDs solution of different concentrations was added to the sol-gel solution and stirred for 40 min. Afterward, the mixtures containing yNCDs were transferred separately to the Teflon-lined autoclave, the other procedures were the same as above.
The following series of ZBH:9%Tm3+/yNCDs are denoted as ZBH:9%Tm3+/yNCDs (1–7), which correspond to the addition amount of yNCDs as 0.03 g, 0.06 g, 0.09 g, 0.12 g, 0.15 g, 0.18 g, 0.21 g.

The ZBH:9%Tm3+/yNCDs (5) sample of the most excellent luminescence performance was coated onto a commercial NUV-LED chip (emitting at 365 nm, operating under 4.0 V), then cured in an oven about 100 °C for 5 h.

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) measurements were performed with Nicolet 6700 ATR-FTIR spectrometer. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded with Bruker D8 Advance X-ray powder diffractometer. The composite samples' morphologies were obtained using a JSM-6711 F scanning electron microscope (SEM) equipped with an energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS). The yNCDs samples' transmission electron microscopic (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images were carried out by a JEM‒2100 microscope. Photoluminescence (PL) and PL excitation (PLE) spectroscopy were measured by Hitachi F-4600 fluorescence spectrometer.

FT-IR spectra and XRD patterns are explored to identify as-obtained samples' structure. FT-IR spectra of ZBH, yNCDs, ZBH:9%Tm3+/yNCDs (1–7) and ZBH:xTm3+ (x = 0–15 mol%) samples, as detected in Fig. 1 and Fig. S1, respectively. For individual yNCDs, the intense band centered at 3442 cm−1 is derived from -OH and -NH stretching vibrations. The absorptions located at 1627, 1506, and 1126 cm−1 are ascribed to stretching vibrations of C═N/C═O, ═˭C, and C─O─C, respectively. The absorption band at 1378 cm−1 is related to the stretching vibrations of C─N [40]. From the above, it can be inferred that the surface of yNCDs ought to contain amino (−NH2) carboxyl (−COOH), and hydroxyl (−OH) groups. As for ZBH:xTm3+ (x = 0–15 mol%) samples (Fig. S1), the FTIR spectrum displays a typical absorption feature of ZBH [41]. The two bands near 3406 and 3360 cm−1 are derived from O─H stretching vibration. The FTIR spectra confirmed the existence of -OH groups in the ZBH:xTm3+ sample. When yNCDs were combined with ZBH:9%Tm3+ to form ZBH:9%Tm3+/yNCDs composite, with the increase of yNCDs, the absorption bands of yNCDs in composites are gradually strengthened, the characteristic absorptions of ZBH are gradually decreased. The FTIR spectra of ZBH:9%Tm3+/yNCDs (1–5) samples still exhibits the typical absorption features of ZBH. However, the absorptions of ZBH:9%Tm3+/yNCDs (6, 7) samples at 3406 cm−1 and 3360 cm−1 become a broad band. It indicates that excessive yNCDs may cause structural changes of ZBH matrix.

To further investigate the structure of ZBH:9%Tm3+/yNCDs composites, the XRD patterns were also analyzed. XRD patterns of ZBH, yNCDs, ZBH:9%Tm3+/yNCDs (1–7) and ZBH:xTm3+ (x = 0–15 mol%) samples are illustrated in Fig. 2 and Fig. S2, respectively. In ZBH:xTm3+ (x = 0–15 mol%) samples, all diffraction peaks can match with 4ZnO·B2O3·H2O pure phase (JCPDS-70–3929), and there are no impurity peaks presented until x ≥ 9 mol%, which indicated that the Tm3+ only replaces part of the Zn2+ and was doped well into the ZBH host lattice. However, the impurity phase peaks seemed more obvious when x > 9 mol% (Fig. S2). It revealed that the phase purity of the sample will be affected when the Tm3+ doping amount is high. Fig. 2 shows that the yNCDs sample consists of a broad peak centered at 2θ = 21.8°, which can correspond to the carbon-based material structure [42,43]. The characteristic diffraction peaks of ZBH:9%Tm3+ and yNCDs can be observed simultaneously from the ZBH:9%Tm3+/yNCDs sample. With the increase of yNCDs, the characteristic peaks of yNCDs in composites are gradually strengthened. When the addition amount of yNCDs was no more than 0.15 g, the main diffraction peaks of the ZBH:9%Tm3+/yNCDs (1–5) are readily indexed to the ZBH crystal phase. At this time, if the addition of yNCDs continues to increase, the main diffraction peaks of ZBH:9%Tm3+/yNCDs (6, 7) samples changed obviously, and many new peaks appeared. It is deduced that the ZBH crystal phase might be destroyed in ZBH:9%Tm3+/yNCDs (6, 7) samples. This result is in good agreement with FT-IR analysis.

The morphologies of the as-prepared ZBH:9%Tm3+/yNCDs (1–7) and ZBH:xTm3+ (x = 0–15 mol%) samples are investigated by SEM, demonstrated in Fig. 3 and Fig. S3, respectively. The ZBH shows the rod-like structure of various sizes. As the Tm3+ concentration gradually increases, the rod-like structure of the ZBH sample tending to form a flake-like structure. When x = 9 mol% in the ZBH:xTm3+ reaction system, the ZBH:9%Tm3+ sample entirely becomes a flake-like morphology. Further, the flake-like structures began to appear pronounced agglomeration, the composite gradually became a composite structure composed of many fragments piled together (Fig. S3). The morphology of yNCDs samples was characterized by TEM (Fig. S4). The TEM image of yNCDs displays relatively uniform spherical particles, with a diameter of approximately 1–3 nm. The HRTEM micrograph (illustration of Fig. S4) of yNCDs samples shows the representative lattice planes with a spacing of about 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) lattice fringes of graphitic carbon [44]. When yNCDs are mixed with ZBH:9%Tm3+, the flake-like structure began to thin (Fig. 3 (1)) and agglomerate (Fig. 3 (2)). With the increase of yNCDs, the flake-like aggregates become larger and larger (Fig. 3 (3)), tends to form micro flower-like structure (Fig. 3 (4)). The final result is the formation of mature flower-like morphology composed of many flake-like structures (Fig. 3 (5)). At the moment, if the addition of yNCDs continues to increase, the flower-like surface will be gradually destroyed (Fig. 3 (6)). Further, the flower-like morphology collapsed, forming irregular aggregates (Fig. 3 (7)). The above facts prove that the morphology of ZBH matrix can be adjusted by doping Tm3+ and yNCDs.

ZBH:9%Tm3+/yNCDs (5) with a typical flower-like structure was selected as the research object of EDS-Mapping, as shown in Fig. 4. The ZBH:9%Tm3+/yNCDs (5) sample presents a uniform and monodispersed flower-like morphology with a diameter of approximately 12 µm. The flower-like morphology is self-assembled with different sizes of thinner flake-like structures. As can be seen from the EDS spectrum, the obtained ZBH:9%Tm3+/yNCDs (5) sample comprises six elements ( B, C, N, O, Zn, and Tm). Besides, mapping analysis clearly reveals that each element is uniformly dispersed in the whole flower-like structure. Meanwhile, the presented results also implies that Tm3+ and yNCDs are effectively doped into the ZBH matrix.

Fig. 5a shows the PLE and PL spectra of ZBH:9%Tm3+ samples. The PLE spectrum of ZBH:9%Tm3+ is a strong excitation band centered at 360 nm, which is mainly derived from 3H6→1D2 electronic transition of Tm3+ [45]. The PL spectrum of the ZBH:9%Tm3+ shows a strong emission band centered near 454 nm, which is the typical PL character band of Tm3+, this is mainly originated from 1D2→3F4 transition [46]. This result indicates that the ZBH:9%Tm3+ may be effectively excited under the NUV light irradiation, matching the current mainstream LED chips (350–420 nm), so that it will have a wider application in luminescent materials field. To obtain the optimal Tm3+ doping amount, the PL spectra of ZBH:xTm3+ (x = 0–15 mol%) samples were studied, as shown in Fig. 5b. When Tm3+ is doped into the ZBH matrix, under the 360 nm excitation wavelength, the ZBH (ZBH:0%Tm3+) matrix exhibits a broad emission band centered at 450 nm, ZBH:xTm3+ shows a strong emission band centered at 454 nm. It is evident that the broad luminescent profiles are observed in ZBH:xTm3+ samples, rather than typical sharp emission bands of Tm3+. At the same time, as the Tm3+ concentration increases within a certain range, the emission of ZBH:xTm3+ (x = 0–15 mol%) shows a trend from broadband to sharp emission band. It can be deduced that the emission band of ZBH:xTm3+ at 454 nm is mainly attributed to the typical 1D2→3F4 transition of Tm3+, but it is affected by the emission of the ZBH matrix to a certain extent. Therefore, the formation of the broad luminescent profiles may be the result of the combined effect of the Tm3+ and ZBH matrix. The illustration in Fig. 5b is the relevance between PL intensity and Tm3+ doping amount during the preparation process. Under 360 nm excitation wavelength, the PL intensity of ZBH:xTm3+ (x = 0–15 mol%) are gradually strengthened until x = 9 mol%, beyond which the emission intensity decreased obviously because of concentration quenching [47]. Therefore, the ZBH:9%Tm3+ sample is identified as the best candidate for producing blue light.

To further optimize PL properties of ZBH:9%Tm3+, the yNCDs were introduced into the ZBH:9%Tm3+ system to form ZBH:9%Tm3+/yNCDs composites. The PL spectra of ZBH:9%Tm3+/yNCDs (1–7) samples under 360 nm characteristic excitation wavelength are collected. As Fig. 6a describes, compared with the ZBH:9%Tm3+, the integrated PL intensity of ZBH:9%Tm3+/yNCDs composite is significantly improved. For the ZBH:9%Tm3+/yNCDs sample, except for the intense blue emission band of Tm3+ at 454 nm, there is also a weak band (478 nm) and a broad band (555 nm) at the same time. According to previous reports, the weak band is derived from 1G4→3H6 transition of Tm3+ [48]. The yellow light broad band emission is most likely associated with core and surface defect-related emission of yNCDs [49,50]. Fig. 6b indicated the relevance between PL intensity of ZBH:Tm3+/yNCDs (1–7) composite and yNCDs doping amount during the preparation process. With the gradual increase of yNCDs, the PL emission of the ZBH:Tm3+/yNCDs (1–7) composite first strengthened and then decreased. The enhancement of luminescence intensity in the first stage may result from energy transfer between yNCDs and Tm3+ ions caused by a rising number of yNCDs [51,52]. The loss of luminescence intensity in the second stage is due to AIQ effect and ZBH structure's destruction [53]. In the analysis of this experiment, ZBH:Tm3+/yNCDs (5) sample has the strongest fluorescence emission. Hence, the obtained ZBH:Tm3+/yNCDs (5) composite is recognized as the most ideal candidate for white light emission.

For the application of developed luminescent materials in WLEDs, the thermal stability of phosphors should be gained enough attention due to the surrounding temperature of the packaged WLEDs device may be relatively high under the working state, the phosphor layer might reach 150 °C [54–56]. The emission spectra of ZBH:9%Tm3+/yNCDs (5) composite undergo a continuous heat test with the temperatures ranging from 50° to 150°C for 12 h were studied, as depicted in Fig. 7. The result revealed that the emission peak positions do not change with the temperature, which is conducive to maintaining stable emitting color at high temperatures (Fig. 7a). Significantly, with the increase of temperature, the PL intensity is affected to varying degrees. The PL spectrum is basically stable below 110 °C, compared with that of at 50 °C, the PL intensity at 150 °C still keeps approximately 72% (Fig. 7b), implying strong resistance to the temperature of the designed ZBH:9%Tm3+/yNCDs (5) composite. Furthermore, the decrease in the PL intensity of the ZBH:9%Tm3+/yNCDs (5) composite may be caused by the fact that the fluorescence structure of yNCDs is easily destroyed under a high-temperature environment, thereby reducing its luminescent activity [57].

The PL data of as-prepared samples at 360 nm excitation wavelength was converted into CIE coordinates ( Fig. 8 and Fig. S5). Significantly, the achieved CIE coordinate of the pure ZBH (ZBH:0%Tm3+) samples was situated in the edge of the blue region and its value was (0.2366, 0.2550), as demonstrated in Fig. S5. The CIE coordinates of as-prepared a series of ZBH:xTm3+ (x = 3–15 mol%) samples are (0.2177, 0.2363), (0.2169, 0.2343), (0.2089, 0.2145), (0.2038, 0.2065), (0.1935, 0.1809), which are populated in blue region. As revealed in the inset of Fig. S5, when the synthesized ZBH:9%Tm3+ samples are exposed in the daylight, the color is white, while they can emit bright blue light upon 365 nm NUV lamp irradiation. It is disclosed in Fig. 8 that the single yNCDs presents a pure yellow emission with CIE coordinates (0.4563, 0.5089). The CIE coordinates of ZBH:9%Tm3+/yNCDs (1–7) are (0.2798, 0.2916), (0.2969, 0.2997), (0.3012, 0.3023), (0.3036, 0.3106), (0.3101, 0.3198), (0.3157, 0.3233), (0.3246, 0.3305). The above analysis results indicate that the luminescence of ZBH matrix can be adjusted by introducing Tm3+ and yNCDs. Finally, the ZBH:9%Tm3+/yNCDs composite with white light emission is obtained. In addition, the WLEDs device was fabricated by combining the ZBH:9%Tm3+/yNCDs (5) sample with a commercial NUV-LED chip emitting under 365 nm ( the inset of Fig. 8). The WLEDs device possesses bright white light emission. It implies that the as-obtained ZBH:9%Tm3+/yNCDs material is likely to the most competitive candidates for WLEDs application. Also, the optimization of material properties and equipment is in further progress.

According to the influence of yNCDs on the morphology of ZBH:9%Tm3+, a possible schematic image of the ZBH:Tm3+/yNCDs composite growth process is proposed and summarized in Scheme 1. Firstly, H3BO3 and ZnSO4.7 H2O are mixed in distilled water, and then ammonia water is added dropwise. Zn2+ and OH- will react immediately to produce Zn(OH)4 2- growth unit, resulting in a low growth rate of crystals. Subsequently, transfer them to a 180 °C environment, Zn(OH)4 2- will interact with H3BO3 to form ZBH rod-like crystal [58]. Secondly, Tm3+ ions are doped into the above system, Tm3+ with a larger ion radius will lower the energy barrier of ZBH, directly affecting the initial formation rate and growth direction of the ZBH crystals. As a result, the morphology of ZBH evolved from rod-like to flake-like. Lastly, the yNCDs solution is introduced into the above reaction. After hydrothermal treatment, the surface groups ( like −OH, −COOH, and −NH2) on the yNCDs interact with the −OH in ZBH:9%Tm3+, the yNCDs were incorporated onto the surface of ZBH:9%Tm3+ flake-like crystals driven by intermolecular forces [59]. In addition, in the seed growth stage, different dopant concentrations will also cause change in crystal phase and morphology [60]. As the reaction proceeded, these flake-like crystals have tightly adhered through self-assembly so that they have the same crystal orientation. Generally, Microspheres shape has the lowest surface energy, reactions are inclined to proceed in the direction of lower energy [61–63]. As a result, the stable ZBH:9%Tm3+/yNCDs flower-like morphology is finally formed through the selective self-assembly of a large number of flake-like crystals. In fact, the actual formation process of flower-like morphology is intricate by reason of many factors may affect self-assembly process, including surface attraction, electrostatic field, dipole field, van der Waals force, hydrophobic interaction, and hydrogen bonding, etc [64–66]. Therefore, the mechanism for the formation of ZBH:9%Tm3+/yNCDs requires additional research.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Correlation of dopant content, composites morphology and luminescence intensity
ce:section.ce:para: It is comprehensively accepted that the fluorescence performance of composites is correlated with the morphology, size, lattice defects, and emission center. Generally, the material with a smaller size will have a larger specific surface area. However, a large specific surface area will increase the lattice defects of the phosphor and reduce its emission intensity. Therefore, when the composite evolves into a flower-like morphology formed by self-assembly of a large number of flake-like crystals, the PL intensity of the phosphor is greatly improved due to the increase of crystal size and the decrease of lattice defects. As a result, the ZBH:9%Tm3+/yNCDs with the largest flower-like structure possess the strongest fluorescence emission. Moreover, as the concentration of dopants increases, the number of emission centers will increase, which will conducive to energy transfer and increase the PL intensity of the composite. Nevertheless, as the concentration of yNCDs increases, the distance between yNCDs in the ZBH:9%Tm3+ matrix decreases, thereby enhancing the aggregation quenching effect between yNCDs. When the concentration of yNCDs reaches saturation, the emission intensity of the ZBH:9%Tm3+/yNCDs will decrease as the concentration of yNCDs increases. Besides, excessive yNCDs can also cause the structural changes of the ZBH compound, which also leads to the decrease of fluorescence intensity of ZBH:9%Tm3+/yNCDs. Consequently, there is a correlation among the dopant content, morphological evolution, and fluorescence properties of ZBH:9%Tm3+/yNCDs composite.

In summary, ZBH:xTm3+ (x = 0–15 mol%) were fabricated by the simple hydrothermal process. When x = 9 mol% in the system, the luminescence intensity of ZBH:9%Tm3+ reaches the maximum value. Further, a series of ZBH:9%Tm3+/yNCDs composite materials with tunable luminescence and morphological were synthesized. Because of being effectively dispersed and protected in the ZBH host, yNCDs successfully overcome the undesirable AIQ effect in solid-state and become the main luminescence center in ZBH:9%Tm3+/yNCDs. What's more, the existence of energy transfer between Tm3+ and yNCDs centers make a great contribution to the PL enhancement of ZBH:9%Tm3+/yNCDs. The experimental results reveal that the flower-like ZBH:9%Tm3+/yNCDs possess the outstanding white light emission and splendid thermal stability under 360 nm excitation wavelength, which nicely matched the current mainstream NUV-LED chip. This investigation provides a feasible and straightforward strategy to enhance luminescence properties of RE-doped materials. Meanwhile, it also beneficial to further expands the application of CDs in white-light sources for solid-state lighting.

Lili He: Formal analysis, Data curation, Methodology, Software, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Rui Liu: Formal analysis, Data curation, Validation, Visualization, Software. Chunhua Ge: Resources, Writing - review & editing, Supervision. Li Yang: Formal analysis, Data curation, Validation, Visualization. Kai Jia: Formal analysis, Data curation, Validation. Xiangdong Zhang: Resources, Writing - review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The present project was supported by Natural Science Foundation of the Education Department of Liaoning Provincial (LJC201916 and LFW201901).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2021.159665.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

Lin, Self-assembled 3D flowerlike Lu2O3 and Lu2O3:Ln3+ (Ln) Eu, Tb, Dy, Pr, Sm, Er, Ho, Tm) microarchitectures: ethylene glycol-Mediated hydrothermal synthesis and luminescent properties, J. Phys. Chem. C 112 (2008) 12777–12785.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Self-assembled 3D flowerlike iron oxide nanostructures and their application in water treatment

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L.S. Zhong, J.Song Hu, H.P. Liang, A.M. Cao, W.G. Song, L.J. Wan, Self-assembled 3D flowerlike iron oxide nanostructures and their application in water treatment, Adv. Mater. 18 (2006) 2426–2431.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Higher-order organization by mesoscale self-assembly and transformation of hybrid nanostructures

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Cölfen, S. Mann, Higher-order organization by mesoscale self-assembly and transformation of hybrid nanostructures, Angew. Chem., Int. Ed. 42 (2003) 2350–2365.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mesocrystals: inorganic superstructures made by highly parallel crystallization and controlled alignment

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Cölfen, M. Antonietti, Mesocrystals: Inorganic superstructures made by highly parallel crystallization and controlled alignment. Angew. Chem., Int. Ed. 44 (2005) 5576–5591.
